1:
"And when Abram was ninety years old and nine, the LORD appeared to Abram, and said unto him, I am the Almighty God; walk before me, and be thou perfect." The witnesses note God appearing as the Almighty and calling Abram to walk before him. On "the LORD appeared to Abram, and said unto him, I am the Almighty God," the grammatical voices note the name. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD appeared... and said unto him, I am the Almighty God (El Shaddai)" — God appeared to Abram (thirteen years after Ishmael's birth, 16:16) and revealed himself by a new name, El Shaddai, "God Almighty" (or "the all-sufficient God"); the name marking God's omnipotence and all-sufficiency, fit to assure Abram (now ninety-nine, with a barren wife) that God could fulfill the promise of a son against all impossibility; the God almighty to do what he had promised.

On "I am the Almighty God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the all-sufficiency. Calvin and Gill note that "El Shaddai" (variously "Almighty," or "the sufficient/all-bountiful One") declares God's power and sufficiency; the name that strengthened Abram's faith — the God who is almighty and all-sufficient could give the promised seed and fulfill the covenant; the ground of faith in the omnipotent God.

On "walk before me, and be thou perfect," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the call: "walk before me, and be thou perfect (upright, sincere, blameless)" — God called Abram to walk before him (in his presence, in faith and obedience) and to be perfect — not sinlessly perfect, but upright, sincere, and wholehearted; the duty of the covenant — to walk before the all-sufficient God in integrity; the response of faith and holiness to the God who reveals himself as Almighty. The verse displays God appearing as the Almighty and calling Abram to walk before him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "I am the Almighty God (El Shaddai)," God revealed as omnipotent and all-sufficient (Keil, Gill); the name strengthening Abram's faith against impossibility (Calvin, Gill); "walk before me, and be thou perfect," the call to upright, sincere, wholehearted obedience (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Almighty God. The verse marks the name: El Shaddai. The verse displays the all-sufficiency: God's. The verse displays the call: walk before me. The verse displays the perfection: upright. The appearing displays God almighty calling Abram to integrity. The verse displays the LORD: appeared. The verse displays the Almighty: revealed. The verse displays the walk: before God. God appearing as the Almighty and calling Abram to walk before him displays the covenant-opening: I am the Almighty God (El Shaddai); walk before me, and be thou perfect — the omnipotent, all-sufficient God, able to fulfill the promise, calling Abram to walk before him in upright, sincere obedience. The verse displays the called Abram, given the covenant (v.2).
2:
"And I will make my covenant between me and thee, and will multiply thee exceedingly." The brief verse notes God's promise to establish his covenant and multiply Abram. The grammatical voices note the covenant. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I will make my covenant between me and thee, and will multiply thee exceedingly" — God declared he would make (establish, confirm) his covenant with Abram, and multiply him exceedingly; the renewal and enlarging of the covenant (15:18); the promise of a numerous posterity, now to be sealed with the sign of circumcision (vv.10-11).

On "I will make my covenant between me and thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the engagement. Calvin and Gill note that God would "make my covenant" — establish his gracious engagement with Abram; the covenant of grace, by which God bound himself to be Abram's God and to give him the seed and the land; the solemn confirming of the covenant.

On "will multiply thee exceedingly," the grammatical-historical and wesleyan-arminian note the multitude: "will multiply thee exceedingly" — the promise of an exceedingly numerous posterity (the great nation, 12:2; the seed as the stars, 15:5); the multiplying of Abram into many nations (v.4); the covenant blessing of fruitfulness; the assurance, to the childless old man, of a vast seed by God's almighty power (v.1). The verse displays God's promise to establish his covenant and multiply Abram.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the covenant — "I will make my covenant between me and thee," God establishing his covenant with Abram (15:18, Keil, Gill); the gracious engagement of the covenant of grace (Calvin, Gill); "will multiply thee exceedingly," the promise of a numerous posterity (12:2; 15:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant. The verse marks the covenant: made. The verse displays the engagement: God's. The verse displays the multiplying: exceeding. The verse displays the seed: promised. The covenant displays God engaging to bless and multiply Abram. The verse displays the covenant: confirmed. The verse displays the seed: multiplied. The verse displays the grace: of God. God's promise to establish his covenant and multiply Abram displays the renewed covenant: I will make my covenant between me and thee, and multiply thee exceedingly — the gracious confirming of the covenant (15:18) and the promise of a vast posterity, by the power of the Almighty (v.1). The verse displays the covenant, before which Abram falls (v.3).
3:
"And Abram fell on his face: and God talked with him, saying," The brief verse notes Abram falling on his face before God. The grammatical voices note the reverence. Gill and Clarke note that "Abram fell on his face: and God talked with him" — Abram, before the appearing of the Almighty (v.1), fell on his face — the posture of profound reverence, awe, and worship; the patriarch prostrate before God; the humility and adoration of Abram in the divine presence; God continued to talk with him (vv.4-21).

On "Abram fell on his face," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the worship. Wesley and Gill note that Abram fell on his face — in reverence and humility before the Almighty; the gesture of worship and submission; the awe of the creature before the Creator; the patriarch's adoration of the God who appeared and spoke.

On "God talked with him, saying," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the condescension: "God talked with him" — God graciously continued to speak with Abram (the prostrate patriarch); the condescension of God to commune with his servant; the familiar dealing of God with Abram (the friend of God, James 2:23; 2 Chronicles 20:7); the revelation of the covenant to the worshiping patriarch. The verse displays Abram falling on his face before God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the reverence — "Abram fell on his face," the posture of awe and worship (Gill, Clarke); the humility and adoration of Abram (Wesley, Gill); "God talked with him," God's condescension to commune with his servant (James 2:23, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prostrate Abram. The verse marks the Abram: on his face. The verse displays the reverence: profound. The verse displays the God: talking. The verse displays the communion: gracious. The reverence displays Abram worshiping before God. The verse displays the Abram: prostrate. The verse displays the worship: offered. The verse displays the God: communing. Abram falling on his face before God displays the reverence of the patriarch: Abram fell on his face, and God talked with him — the patriarch prostrate in awe and worship before the Almighty, and God graciously communing with his servant. The verse displays the worshiping Abram, made father of many nations (v.4).
4:
"As for me, behold, my covenant is with thee, and thou shalt be a father of many nations." The witnesses note the promise that Abram shall be a father of many nations. On "As for me, behold, my covenant is with thee," the grammatical voices note God's engagement. Ellicott and Gill note that "As for me, behold, my covenant is with thee" — God declared his part in the covenant: his gracious engagement with Abram; "as for me" marks God's side of the covenant (Abram's side being to keep it, v.9); the LORD binding himself to Abram by covenant.

On "thou shalt be a father of many nations," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the promise. Calvin and Gill note that "thou shalt be a father of many nations" — Abram would be the father of many nations: not Israel only, but many peoples (the Ishmaelites, the children of Keturah, the Edomites, and, spiritually, all the faithful of every nation, Romans 4:11-12, 16-18, the father of all who believe); the wide fulfillment of the promise; Abram the father of a multitude.

On "father of many nations," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the spiritual fatherhood: the promise embraces both Abram's natural descendants (the many nations of his seed) and his spiritual children (the believing Gentiles, who are Abraham's seed by faith, Galatians 3:7, 29); Abram the father of the faithful; the new name (v.5) sealing this fatherhood of many nations. The verse displays the promise that Abram shall be a father of many nations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the engagement — "As for me, behold, my covenant is with thee," God's side of the covenant (Ellicott, Gill); "thou shalt be a father of many nations," Abram the father of many peoples, natural and spiritual (Romans 4:11-18, Calvin, Gill); the spiritual fatherhood of all believers (Galatians 3:7, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised fatherhood. The verse marks the covenant: God's. The verse displays the father: of many nations. The verse displays the seed: natural and spiritual. The verse displays the faithful: his children. The promise displays Abram the father of a multitude. The verse displays the covenant: God's part. The verse displays the father: of nations. The verse displays the fatherhood: wide. The promise that Abram shall be a father of many nations displays the wide covenant blessing: my covenant is with thee, and thou shalt be a father of many nations — Abram the father of many peoples, his natural seed and (by faith) all the faithful of every nation (Romans 4:11-18; Galatians 3:7, 29). The verse displays the father of nations, renamed Abraham (v.5).
5:
"Neither shall thy name any more be called Abram, but thy name shall be Abraham; for a father of many nations have I made thee." The witnesses note the changing of Abram's name to Abraham. On "neither shall thy name any more be called Abram, but thy name shall be Abraham," the grammatical voices note the new name. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "thy name shall be Abraham" — God changed Abram's name (Abram, "high/exalted father") to Abraham ("father of a multitude"); the new name, given by God, marking and sealing the covenant promise; the change of name a token of the new relation and the new promise; the patriarch renamed for his destiny.

On "for a father of many nations have I made thee," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the meaning. Clarke and Ellicott note that "Abraham" means "father of a multitude" (ab-hamon, father of many) — the name embodying the promise (v.4); "have I made thee" — God speaks of it as already done (the certainty of the promise); Abraham, the father of many nations, by God's appointment; the name a perpetual witness of the covenant.

On the new name, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the significance: the changing of the name (as later Sarai to Sarah, v.15, and Jacob to Israel, 32:28) marks a new stage in God's dealing and a new identity under the covenant; the name Abraham would be a standing memorial of the promise; the patriarch bearing in his very name the assurance that he would be the father of many nations; the seal of the covenant in the new name. The verse displays the changing of Abram's name to Abraham.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the new name — "thy name shall be Abraham," Abram renamed Abraham, "father of a multitude" (Keil, Gill); "for a father of many nations have I made thee," the name embodying the promise, as good as done (Clarke, Ellicott); the new name a memorial and seal of the covenant (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renamed Abraham. The verse marks the name: changed. The verse displays the Abraham: father of a multitude. The verse displays the promise: embodied. The verse displays the seal: of the covenant. The renaming displays Abraham marked for his destiny. The verse displays the Abram: renamed. The verse displays the name: Abraham. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The changing of Abram's name to Abraham displays the seal of the promise: thy name shall be Abraham, for a father of many nations have I made thee — the patriarch renamed "father of a multitude," the new name a standing memorial and seal of the covenant promise. The verse displays the renamed Abraham, made exceeding fruitful (v.6).
6:
"And I will make thee exceeding fruitful, and I will make nations of thee, and kings shall come out of thee." The very brief verse notes the promise of fruitfulness, nations, and kings from Abraham. The grammatical voices note the threefold promise. Gill notes that "I will make thee exceeding fruitful, and I will make nations of thee, and kings shall come out of thee" — God promised Abraham exceeding fruitfulness (a vast posterity), nations (many peoples descending from him), and kings (royal descendants); the enlarging of the promise; the patriarch the source of nations and kings.

On "I will make nations of thee," the witnesses note the peoples. Gill notes that "nations" would come of Abraham — Israel, and the other peoples of his seed (Ishmaelites, Edomites, the sons of Keturah); the many nations descending from the one man; the fulfillment of the name Abraham (v.5).

On "kings shall come out of thee," the grammatical-historical note the royalty: "kings shall come out of thee" — kings would descend from Abraham (the kings of Israel and Judah, of Edom, and others; and supremely the King, the Messiah, Christ, of Abraham's line, Matthew 1:1); the royal promise; the patriarch the ancestor of kings, and of the King of kings; the dignity of Abraham's seed. The verse displays the promise of fruitfulness, nations, and kings.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "I will make thee exceeding fruitful, and I will make nations of thee, and kings shall come out of thee," the threefold promise of fruitfulness, nations, and kings (Gill); "I will make nations of thee," the many peoples of his seed (Gill); "kings shall come out of thee," the royal descendants, and the King, Christ (Matthew 1:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised seed. The verse marks the fruitfulness: exceeding. The verse displays the nations: of Abraham. The verse displays the kings: from him. The verse displays the Messiah: the King. The promise displays Abraham the source of nations and kings. The verse displays the seed: fruitful. The verse displays the nations: many. The verse displays the kings: royal. The promise of fruitfulness, nations, and kings from Abraham displays the enlarged covenant: I will make thee exceeding fruitful, make nations of thee, and kings shall come out of thee — the patriarch the source of a vast posterity, many nations, and kings (and supremely the King, the Messiah, Matthew 1:1). The verse displays the fruitful Abraham, given the everlasting covenant (v.7).
7:
"And I will establish my covenant between me and thee and thy seed after thee in their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee." The witnesses note the everlasting covenant, "to be a God unto thee and to thy seed." On "I will establish my covenant between me and thee and thy seed after thee in their generations for an everlasting covenant," the grammatical voices note the perpetuity. Gill and Henry note that "I will establish my covenant... for an everlasting covenant" — God established his covenant with Abraham and his seed throughout their generations, as an everlasting covenant; the covenant perpetual, enduring (in its substance, the covenant of grace) to all generations and forever; the unbreakable, eternal engagement of God.

On "to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the heart of the covenant. Calvin and Gill note that the substance of the covenant is "to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee" — God himself, given to Abraham and his seed as their God; the highest blessing — that the LORD would be their God, their portion, their salvation; the covenant relation (I will be their God, and they shall be my people, Jeremiah 31:33; the formula of the covenant of grace); the heart of all the promises — God himself the believer's God.

On the everlasting covenant, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the grace: the everlasting covenant, "to be a God" to Abraham and his seed, is the covenant of grace, fulfilled in Christ and extending to all the faithful (Abraham's spiritual seed, Galatians 3:29); God's gracious, perpetual engagement to be the God of his people; the foundation of the believer's hope and salvation; the covenant that secures God himself as the portion of his people forever. The verse displays the everlasting covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the perpetuity — "I will establish my covenant... for an everlasting covenant," the perpetual covenant of grace (Gill, Henry); "to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee," God himself the portion of his people, the heart of the covenant (Jeremiah 31:33, Calvin, Gill); the covenant of grace fulfilled in Christ, extending to the faithful (Galatians 3:29, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the everlasting covenant. The verse marks the covenant: everlasting. The verse displays the God: to be theirs. The verse displays the seed: included. The verse displays the grace: perpetual. The covenant displays God engaging to be the God of his people forever. The verse displays the covenant: eternal. The verse displays the God: given. The verse displays the people: his. The everlasting covenant, "to be a God unto thee and to thy seed," displays the heart of the covenant of grace: I will establish my everlasting covenant, to be a God unto thee and to thy seed — God himself given to Abraham and his seed as their God, the highest blessing, the perpetual covenant of grace fulfilled in Christ (Galatians 3:29). The verse displays the covenant God, granting the land (v.8).
8:
"And I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee, the land wherein thou art a stranger, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession; and I will be their God." The witnesses note the grant of the land of Canaan for an everlasting possession. On "I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee... all the land of Canaan," the grammatical voices note the land-grant. Gill and Clarke note that "I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee, the land wherein thou art a stranger, all the land of Canaan" — God granted the land of Canaan (in which Abraham was now a stranger and sojourner, Hebrews 11:9, possessing none of it, Acts 7:5) to Abraham and his seed; the land-promise (12:7; 15:18) renewed and confirmed; the inheritance assured to his descendants.

On "for an everlasting possession," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the perpetuity. Calvin and Gill note that the land was given "for an everlasting possession" — the perpetual grant of Canaan to Abraham's seed (Israel, for the appointed age; and, typologically, the heavenly inheritance, the everlasting rest, Hebrews 11:10, 16, of which Canaan was a type); the land the everlasting possession of the covenant-people.

On "I will be their God," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the covenant relation: "I will be their God" — the repeated heart of the covenant (v.7) — God himself the God of Abraham's seed; the land-promise sealed with the covenant relation; the inheritance of the land joined to the inheritance of God himself; the covenant of grace, God the portion of his people. The verse displays the grant of the land for an everlasting possession.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the land-grant — "I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee... all the land of Canaan," the land granted to Abraham and his seed (12:7; 15:18; Hebrews 11:9, Gill, Clarke); "for an everlasting possession," the perpetual grant (and the heavenly type, Hebrews 11:10, Calvin, Gill); "I will be their God," the covenant relation sealing the grant (v.7, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the granted land. The verse marks the land: of Canaan. The verse displays the possession: everlasting. The verse displays the stranger: Abraham. The verse displays the God: theirs. The grant displays God giving the land and himself to his people. The verse displays the land: given. The verse displays the possession: perpetual. The verse displays the God: their portion. The grant of the land for an everlasting possession displays the covenant inheritance: I will give all the land of Canaan for an everlasting possession, and I will be their God — the land (a type of the heavenly inheritance, Hebrews 11:10) granted to Abraham's seed, joined to the gift of God himself as their God. The verse displays the granted land, with the duty to keep the covenant (v.9).
9:
"And God said unto Abraham, Thou shalt keep my covenant therefore, thou, and thy seed after thee in their generations." The brief verse notes the duty to keep the covenant. The grammatical voices note Abraham's part. Gill and Calvin note that "God said unto Abraham, Thou shalt keep my covenant therefore, thou, and thy seed after thee in their generations" — having declared his own part (vv.4-8), God now charged Abraham with his part: to keep the covenant; the duty of the covenant-people, to observe and keep God's covenant (and its sign, circumcision, vv.10-14); the response of obedience to God's grace.

On "Thou shalt keep my covenant," the reformed Calvin notes the duty. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham and his seed were to "keep my covenant" — to observe its terms, especially the sign of circumcision (vv.10-11); the obligation of the covenant-people; not that the covenant rested on man's keeping (it is God's gracious engagement), but that those in covenant must keep its sign and walk before God (v.1).

On "thou, and thy seed after thee in their generations," the grammatical-historical note the perpetuity: the duty bound Abraham and his seed throughout their generations; the keeping of the covenant (and circumcision) a perpetual obligation of the covenant-people; the response of faith and obedience to God's everlasting covenant; the covenant kept, generation after generation, in its appointed sign. The verse displays the duty to keep the covenant.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the duty — "Thou shalt keep my covenant therefore, thou, and thy seed after thee," Abraham's part, to keep the covenant (Gill, Calvin); "thou shalt keep my covenant," the obligation of the covenant-people, especially circumcision (vv.10-11, Calvin, Gill); the perpetual duty through the generations (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charged Abraham. The verse marks the duty: keep the covenant. The verse displays the seed: bound. The verse displays the obedience: required. The verse displays the sign: to come. The charge displays God binding Abraham to keep the covenant. The verse displays the Abraham: charged. The verse displays the covenant: kept. The verse displays the obedience: pressed. The duty to keep the covenant displays Abraham's part: thou shalt keep my covenant, thou and thy seed in their generations — the response of obedience to God's grace, the covenant-people bound to keep the covenant and its sign (vv.10-11). The verse displays the charged Abraham, given the sign of circumcision (v.10).
10:
"This is my covenant, which ye shall keep, between me and you and thy seed after thee; Every man child among you shall be circumcised." The witnesses note the institution of circumcision as the covenant-sign. On "This is my covenant, which ye shall keep," the grammatical voices note the appointment. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "This is my covenant, which ye shall keep... Every man child among you shall be circumcised" — God appointed circumcision as the sign and obligation of the covenant; "this is my covenant" (that is, the sign of it) which Abraham's house must keep; the institution of circumcision, the token of the covenant (v.11), to be observed by all the males.

On "Every man child among you shall be circumcised," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the rite. Clarke and Ellicott note that every male among Abraham's seed and household was to be circumcised — the cutting of the foreskin (v.11); the distinguishing mark of the covenant-people; the rite by which a male was admitted into the covenant of Abraham; the sign borne in the flesh.

On the institution, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the significance: circumcision, the appointed sign, was the seal of the covenant (Romans 4:11) and the badge of the covenant-people; it signified the covenant relation and (in its spiritual import, v.11) the circumcision of the heart; the sign instituted for Abraham and his seed; the outward token of the inward covenant. The verse displays the institution of circumcision.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appointment — "This is my covenant... Every man child among you shall be circumcised," circumcision appointed as the covenant-sign (Keil, Gill); the rite of every male, the mark of the covenant-people (Clarke, Ellicott); the sign and seal of the covenant (Romans 4:11, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appointed sign. The verse marks the covenant: its sign. The verse displays the circumcision: of every male. The verse displays the people: marked. The verse displays the seal: of the covenant. The institution displays circumcision the token of the covenant. The verse displays the sign: appointed. The verse displays the males: circumcised. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The institution of circumcision as the covenant-sign displays the appointed token: this is my covenant, every man child shall be circumcised — circumcision instituted as the sign and seal of the covenant (Romans 4:11), the badge of the covenant-people, borne in the flesh. The verse displays the appointed circumcision, defined in the flesh (v.11).
11:
"And ye shall circumcise the flesh of your foreskin; and it shall be a token of the covenant betwixt me and you." The witnesses note circumcision as the token of the covenant — the crux of its significance. On "ye shall circumcise the flesh of your foreskin," the grammatical voices note the rite. Gill and Clarke note that "ye shall circumcise the flesh of your foreskin" — the rite of circumcision, the cutting off of the foreskin; the bodily sign appointed for the covenant-people; the mark borne in the flesh.

On "it shall be a token of the covenant," interpreters expound the significance. The reformed Calvin and Gill note that circumcision "shall be a token (sign) of the covenant" — the outward sign and seal of the covenant of grace; Paul declares it "a seal of the righteousness of the faith" which Abraham had (Romans 4:11) — that is, circumcision sealed and confirmed the righteousness Abraham already had by faith (15:6, before he was circumcised); the sign attesting and ratifying the covenant promise and the righteousness of faith. The sign also signified, in its spiritual intention, the circumcision of the heart — the putting off of the body of the flesh, the mortifying of sin, regeneration (Deuteronomy 10:16; 30:6; Jeremiah 4:4; Romans 2:28-29; Colossians 2:11); the cutting away of the flesh a figure of the spiritual cleansing and renewal of the heart; the outward sign of an inward grace.

On the token, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the import: circumcision was a token, a seal, and a figure — the badge of the covenant-people, the seal of the righteousness of faith (Romans 4:11), and the type of the circumcision of the heart (Colossians 2:11); not a mere fleshly or national mark, but a sign with deep spiritual significance, pointing to the inward grace of regeneration and the righteousness of faith. The verse displays circumcision as the token of the covenant.

The witnesses agree the substance: circumcision is the token (sign and seal) of the covenant (Gill, Calvin, Clarke). Its significance is its weight as a crux: the outward sign and seal of the covenant and of the righteousness of faith (Romans 4:11), signifying in its spiritual intention the circumcision of the heart — regeneration, the putting off of the flesh (Deuteronomy 30:6; Colossians 2:11) — and so not a mere fleshly/national mark but the sign of an inward grace. The verse displays the circumcision-significance crux. The verse marks the rite: of the foreskin. The verse displays the token: of the covenant. The verse displays the seal: of righteousness by faith. The verse displays the heart: circumcised. The token displays the outward sign of the inward grace. The verse displays the sign: appointed. The verse displays the seal: of faith's righteousness. The verse displays the heart: its circumcision. Circumcision as the token of the covenant displays its significance and a crux: it shall be a token of the covenant — the outward sign and seal of the covenant and of the righteousness of faith (Romans 4:11), signifying the circumcision of the heart, regeneration and the putting off of the flesh (Deuteronomy 30:6; Colossians 2:11), the sign of an inward grace. The verse displays the appointed token, for every male (vv.12-13).
12:
"And he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you, every man child in your generations, he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any stranger, which is not of thy seed." The witnesses note the circumcision of every male on the eighth day. On "he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you," the grammatical voices note the time. Ellicott and Gill note that "he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you" — every male was to be circumcised on the eighth day after birth; the appointed time for the rite (later the day of Christ's circumcision, Luke 2:21, and of naming); the infant brought into the covenant on the eighth day; the time fixed by God.

On "every man child in your generations," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the perpetuity. Calvin and Gill note that "every man child in your generations" was to be circumcised — the rite perpetual, binding every male of Abraham's seed throughout the generations; the standing obligation of the covenant-people; the sign borne by every male.

On "he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any stranger, which is not of thy seed," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the household: the rite extended beyond Abraham's natural seed to "he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any stranger" — the home-born servants and the purchased slaves, even those not of Abraham's blood; all the males of the household were to be circumcised and so brought into the covenant; the sign embracing the whole household of faith, not the natural seed only; a foreshadowing of the Gentiles' incorporation into the covenant. The verse displays the circumcision of every male on the eighth day.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the time — "he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you," the rite on the eighth day (Luke 2:21, Ellicott, Gill); "every man child in your generations," the perpetual obligation (Calvin, Gill); "he that is born in the house, or bought with money," the whole household, even servants, circumcised (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appointed rite. The verse marks the day: eighth. The verse displays the males: all. The verse displays the household: included. The verse displays the covenant: extended. The rite displays every male of the household brought into the covenant. The verse displays the day: eighth. The verse displays the seed: and servants. The verse displays the sign: borne. The circumcision of every male on the eighth day displays the appointed rite: every male, on the eighth day, the home-born and the bought, shall be circumcised — the sign borne by every male of Abraham's household, natural seed and servants alike, brought into the covenant. The verse displays the household rite, a perpetual covenant (v.13).
13:
"He that is born in thy house, and he that is bought with thy money, must needs be circumcised: and my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant." The brief verse notes that all the household must be circumcised, the covenant in their flesh. The grammatical voices note the obligation. Gill and Clarke note that "He that is born in thy house, and he that is bought with thy money, must needs be circumcised" — all the males of the household (home-born and purchased) must be circumcised; the rite obligatory for the whole household; the emphatic "must needs be" marking the necessity; the sign borne by all the males of Abraham's house.

On "born in thy house... bought with thy money," the witnesses note the household. Gill notes that the rite embraced both the home-born servants and the bought slaves — the whole household, not the natural seed only; all brought into the covenant by the sign; the embracing of the household of faith.

On "my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the perpetual sign: "my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant" — the covenant-sign borne in the flesh, a perpetual token; circumcision, the abiding mark of the covenant in the body; the everlasting covenant sealed in the flesh of the covenant-people; the sign that would distinguish Abraham's seed (until its fulfillment and abrogation in Christ, Galatians 5:6; Colossians 2:11). The verse displays that all the household must be circumcised.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obligation — "He that is born in thy house, and he that is bought with thy money, must needs be circumcised," all the household circumcised (Gill, Clarke); the whole household, not the seed only (Gill); "my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant," the perpetual sign in the flesh (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the household sign. The verse marks the household: all circumcised. The verse displays the necessity: must needs. The verse displays the covenant: in the flesh. The verse displays the sign: everlasting. The obligation displays the whole household marked with the covenant-sign. The verse displays the household: circumcised. The verse displays the flesh: marked. The verse displays the covenant: perpetual. That all the household must be circumcised displays the covenant borne in the flesh: the home-born and the bought must be circumcised, and my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant — the whole household marked with the perpetual sign of the covenant, borne in the flesh. The verse displays the household sign, with the penalty for neglect (v.14).
14:
"And the uncircumcised man child whose flesh of his foreskin is not circumcised, that soul shall be cut off from his people; he hath broken my covenant." The witnesses note the penalty of being cut off for neglecting circumcision. On "the uncircumcised man child... that soul shall be cut off from his people," the grammatical voices note the penalty. Gill and Ellicott note that "the uncircumcised man child whose flesh of his foreskin is not circumcised, that soul shall be cut off from his people" — the male who was not circumcised (by his own neglect, or by his parents' default, when grown to be answerable) was to be "cut off from his people"; the penalty for despising or neglecting the covenant-sign; the uncircumcised excluded from the covenant-people.

On "that soul shall be cut off," interpreters note the meaning. Calvin and Clarke note that "cut off" is variously understood — excommunicated, cut off from the congregation and privileges of the covenant-people; or cut off by death (a divine judgment, or the penalty of the law); the exclusion from the covenant and its blessings; the severing of the neglecter from the people of God.

On "he hath broken my covenant," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the offense: "he hath broken my covenant" — the neglect of the sign was a breaking of the covenant; to despise the token was to despise the covenant itself; the contempt of the sign was contempt of God's gracious engagement; the seriousness of neglecting the covenant-sign; the warning to the covenant-people to keep the sign in faith. The verse displays the penalty of being cut off for neglecting circumcision.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the penalty — "the uncircumcised man child... that soul shall be cut off from his people," the cutting off of the neglecter (Gill, Ellicott); "cut off," excommunication or death, exclusion from the covenant (Calvin, Clarke); "he hath broken my covenant," the neglect of the sign a breaking of the covenant (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the penalty. The verse marks the uncircumcised: cut off. The verse displays the cutting off: excommunication or death. The verse displays the covenant: broken. The verse displays the warning: to keep the sign. The penalty displays the neglecter severed from the covenant-people. The verse displays the uncircumcised: excluded. The verse displays the covenant: despised. The verse displays the warning: solemn. The penalty of being cut off for neglecting circumcision displays the seriousness of the sign: the uncircumcised shall be cut off, for he hath broken my covenant — the neglecter excluded from the covenant-people (by excommunication or death), the contempt of the sign a breaking of the covenant. The verse displays the penalty, before the renaming of Sarah (v.15).
15:
"And God said unto Abraham, As for Sarai thy wife, thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be." The witnesses note the changing of Sarai's name to Sarah. On "thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be," the grammatical voices note the new name. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be" — God changed Sarai's name to Sarah; the names are close in form (both relating to "princess/noblewoman"), Sarah meaning "princess" (perhaps "princess of a multitude," fitting the promise that nations and kings would come of her, v.16); the new name, like Abraham's (v.5), marking and sealing her share in the covenant promise.

On "Sarai... Sarah," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the meaning. Clarke and Ellicott note that the change from Sarai to Sarah marks her new dignity — "Sarah," a princess, the mother of nations and kings (v.16); the matriarch given a name befitting the promise; the wife included, by name, in the covenant blessing.

On the renaming, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the inclusion of Sarah: the changing of Sarah's name (as Abraham's, v.5) marks that the covenant promise of the seed was through her — not through Hagar or another, but through Sarah, the wife; the matriarch of the promised seed; the new name sealing her place as the mother of the covenant-line (Isaac, v.19); Sarah included in the covenant and its promise. The verse displays the changing of Sarai's name to Sarah.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the new name — "Sarah shall her name be," Sarai renamed Sarah, "princess" (Keil, Gill); the name marking her dignity as mother of nations and kings (v.16, Clarke, Ellicott); the renaming sealing her place as mother of the promised seed (v.19, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renamed Sarah. The verse marks the name: changed. The verse displays the Sarah: princess. The verse displays the mother: of nations. The verse displays the covenant: through her. The renaming displays Sarah included in the covenant promise. The verse displays the Sarai: renamed. The verse displays the Sarah: princess. The verse displays the promise: through her. The changing of Sarai's name to Sarah displays her place in the covenant: thou shalt call her Sarah — the matriarch renamed "princess," the mother of nations and kings (v.16), the covenant promise of the seed through her, not another. The verse displays the renamed Sarah, blessed with a son (v.16).
16:
"And I will bless her, and give thee a son also of her: yea, I will bless her, and she shall be a mother of nations; kings of people shall be of her." The witnesses note the promise to bless Sarah with a son and make her a mother of nations. On "I will bless her, and give thee a son also of her," the grammatical voices note the promise. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I will bless her, and give thee a son also of her" — God promised to bless Sarah and give Abraham a son by her (Isaac, v.19); the express promise that the seed would be through Sarah, not Hagar; the matriarch, barren and old, to bear the son of promise by God's blessing; the human impossibility met by the divine promise.

On "she shall be a mother of nations; kings of people shall be of her," the reformed and witnesses note the dignity. Ellicott and Gill note that "she shall be a mother of nations; kings of people shall be of her" — Sarah would be the mother of nations and the ancestress of kings (as Abraham, vv.4-6); the matriarch of the covenant-line, through whom would come Israel and the kings (and the King, Christ); the dignity of Sarah in the covenant; the promise fulfilled in Isaac and his seed.

On the blessing, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the emphasis: the repeated "I will bless her" marks the certainty and fullness of the blessing on Sarah; the once-barren, now-honored matriarch; the mother of the promised seed, of nations, and of kings; the covenant blessing resting on Sarah as on Abraham. The verse displays the promise to bless Sarah with a son.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "I will bless her, and give thee a son also of her," the son of promise through Sarah (Isaac, v.19, Keil, Gill); "she shall be a mother of nations; kings of people shall be of her," Sarah the mother of nations and kings (Ellicott, Gill); the repeated blessing marking its certainty (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed Sarah. The verse marks the blessing: on Sarah. The verse displays the son: given. The verse displays the mother: of nations. The verse displays the kings: of her. The promise displays Sarah blessed as mother of the promised seed. The verse displays the Sarah: blessed. The verse displays the son: promised. The verse displays the nations: from her. The promise to bless Sarah with a son and make her a mother of nations displays her place in the covenant: I will bless her and give thee a son of her; she shall be a mother of nations, and kings shall be of her — the once-barren matriarch to bear the son of promise (Isaac), the mother of nations and kings (and the King, Christ). The verse displays the blessed Sarah, at which Abraham laughs (v.17).
17:
"Then Abraham fell upon his face, and laughed, and said in his heart, Shall a child be born unto him that is an hundred years old? and shall Sarah, that is ninety years old, bear?" The witnesses note Abraham's laughter at the promise — the crux of its meaning. On "Abraham fell upon his face, and laughed," interpreters divide over the laughter. The dominant reading (the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and many) takes it as the laughter of joy and faith: Abraham, falling on his face in worship, laughed for joy and wonder at the greatness of the promise; he believed (Romans 4:19-20 testifies that Abraham "staggered not at the promise through unbelief, but was strong in faith... being fully persuaded"); his laughter was the rejoicing of faith (cf. John 8:56, "Abraham rejoiced to see my day"); the holy joy and admiration of the believing patriarch, not the doubt of unbelief. A second reading takes the laughter as mixed with a momentary wonder or weakness — a natural astonishment at the seeming impossibility (a hundred-year-old man and a ninety-year-old woman bearing a child), expressing both faith and a touch of human marveling, though not the culpable unbelief of which Sarah's laugh (18:12) is more nearly reproved.

On "Shall a child be born unto him that is an hundred years old? and shall Sarah, that is ninety years old, bear?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the wonder. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham's words ("Shall a child be born unto him that is a hundred years old?") express the wonder of the thing — the natural impossibility against which the promise stood; yet (on the dominant reading) the wonder was the marvel of faith, not the objection of unbelief; Abraham, considering the impossibility, yet believed God who quickens the dead (Romans 4:17-21).

On the laughter, the grammatical-historical note the contrast: Abraham's laugh (the laugh of joyful faith) is to be distinguished from Sarah's laugh (18:12, the laugh of incredulous wonder, which was reproved); Isaac ("laughter," 21:6) was named for the joy; the holy joy of the believing patriarch at the promise of the son. The verse displays Abraham's laughter at the promise.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham fell on his face, laughed, and wondered at the promise of a son to a hundred-year-old man and a ninety-year-old woman (Clarke, Calvin, Gill). The laughter bears a crux: the laughter of joy and faith — Abraham rejoicing and strong in faith (the dominant reading, Romans 4:19-20; John 8:56) vs a laughter mixed with a momentary wonder or human weakness at the seeming impossibility (though not the culpable unbelief of Sarah's laugh, 18:12). The verse displays the laughter crux. The verse marks the laughter: Abraham's. The verse displays the debate: joy/faith or wonder/weakness. The verse displays the wonder: at the impossibility. The verse displays the faith: strong (Romans 4). The laughter displays Abraham's response to the promise. The verse displays the Abraham: laughing. The verse displays the joy: of faith. The verse displays the promise: marveled at. Abraham's laughter at the promise displays his response and a crux: Abraham fell on his face and laughed — read as the laughter of joy and faith, Abraham strong in faith and rejoicing (the dominant reading, Romans 4:19-20; John 8:56), or as a laughter mixed with momentary wonder at the impossibility; distinguished from Sarah's reproved laugh (18:12). The verse displays the laughing Abraham, pleading for Ishmael (v.18).
18:
"And Abraham said unto God, O that Ishmael might live before thee!" The brief verse notes Abraham's plea for Ishmael. The grammatical voices note the prayer. Gill and Clarke note that "Abraham said unto God, O that Ishmael might live before thee!" — Abraham, in his joy and his love for his son Ishmael (then thirteen), pleaded that Ishmael might live before God — that he might share in the covenant blessing, or be the heir; the patriarch's affection for Ishmael; the prayer that Ishmael might not be passed over.

On "O that Ishmael might live before thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the affection. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham's plea sprang from his fatherly love for Ishmael; perhaps a fear that the promise of a new son meant Ishmael's exclusion; the patriarch desiring that Ishmael, his firstborn, might live and be blessed before God; the natural affection of the father, brought to God in prayer.

On the plea, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the answer to come: Abraham's prayer for Ishmael would be answered — God would bless Ishmael (v.20), though the covenant would be established with Isaac (v.21); the patriarch's affection met with God's gracious response; the love for Ishmael not unregarded, yet the covenant-line through Isaac. The verse displays Abraham's plea for Ishmael.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the prayer — "O that Ishmael might live before thee," Abraham's plea for his son Ishmael (Gill, Clarke); the fatherly affection for Ishmael (Calvin, Gill); the prayer answered in God's blessing of Ishmael (v.20, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pleading Abraham. The verse marks the plea: for Ishmael. The verse displays the affection: fatherly. The verse displays the Ishmael: his son. The verse displays the answer: to come. The plea displays Abraham's love for Ishmael brought to God. The verse displays the Abraham: praying. The verse displays the Ishmael: beloved. The verse displays the affection: shown. Abraham's plea for Ishmael displays the father's affection: O that Ishmael might live before thee! — the patriarch, loving his firstborn, pleading that Ishmael might live and be blessed before God, a prayer God would answer (v.20), though the covenant be with Isaac. The verse displays the pleading Abraham, answered concerning Isaac (v.19).
19:
"And God said, Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son indeed; and thou shalt call his name Isaac: and I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant, and with his seed after him." The witnesses note the promise of Isaac, the son of the covenant. On "Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son indeed," the grammatical voices note the promise. Ellicott and Gill note that "Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son indeed" — God affirmed that Sarah herself (not Hagar) would bear Abraham a son; "indeed" (in truth, certainly) marks the certainty against the impossibility (v.17); the son of promise to be born of Sarah; the express confirming of the promise.

On "thou shalt call his name Isaac," the wesleyan-arminian and witnesses note the name. Gill notes that the son was to be named Isaac ("he laughs/laughter") — the name commemorating Abraham's joyful laugh (v.17) at the promise (and Sarah's, 18:12; 21:6); the son of laughter, of joy; the name given by God before the birth.

On "I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant, and with his seed after him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the covenant-line: "I will establish my covenant with him" — the covenant (and the line of promise) would be established with Isaac, not Ishmael; Isaac the son of the covenant, through whom the promised seed (and the Messiah) would come (Romans 9:7; Galatians 4:28, the children of promise); the covenant-line fixed in Isaac; the everlasting covenant with Isaac and his seed. The verse displays the promise of Isaac, the son of the covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son indeed," the son of promise born of Sarah, certain against impossibility (Ellicott, Gill); "thou shalt call his name Isaac," the son of laughter/joy (v.17; 21:6, Gill); "I will establish my covenant with him," the covenant-line through Isaac, not Ishmael (Romans 9:7, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised Isaac. The verse marks the son: Isaac. The verse displays the Sarah: shall bear. The verse displays the covenant: with Isaac. The verse displays the line: of promise. The promise displays Isaac the son of the covenant. The verse displays the Isaac: named. The verse displays the covenant: established. The verse displays the seed: of promise. The promise of Isaac, the son of the covenant, displays the fixing of the line: Sarah shall bear a son indeed, and thou shalt call him Isaac, and I will establish my covenant with him — Isaac the son of promise, born of Sarah, with whom the everlasting covenant (and the line of the Messiah) would be established, not Ishmael (Romans 9:7). The verse displays the promised Isaac, with the blessing of Ishmael (v.20).
20:
"And as for Ishmael, I have heard thee: Behold, I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful, and will multiply him exceedingly; twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation." The witnesses note the blessing of Ishmael in answer to Abraham's prayer. On "as for Ishmael, I have heard thee," the grammatical voices note the answer. Gill and Clarke note that "as for Ishmael, I have heard thee" — God answered Abraham's plea (v.18) for Ishmael; the prayer of the father heard; God's gracious regard to Abraham's affection; Ishmael not unblessed, though not the covenant-heir.

On "I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful, and will multiply him exceedingly," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the temporal blessing. Calvin and Gill note that God blessed Ishmael — making him fruitful and multiplying him exceedingly; the temporal blessing on Ishmael (a numerous posterity, the Arab peoples, 16:10; 25:12-18); the kindness of God to Ishmael for Abraham's sake.

On "twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the great nation: "twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation" — Ishmael would beget twelve princes (the twelve sons/tribes, 25:12-16) and become a great nation (the Arab peoples); the temporal greatness of Ishmael; yet (v.21) the covenant would be with Isaac; the blessing on Ishmael great, but distinct from the covenant-line. The verse displays the blessing of Ishmael.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the answer — "as for Ishmael, I have heard thee," God answering Abraham's prayer (v.18, Gill, Clarke); "I have blessed him... will multiply him exceedingly," the temporal blessing, a numerous posterity (16:10; 25:12-18, Calvin, Gill); "twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation," the twelve tribes and the great nation (25:12-16, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed Ishmael. The verse marks the prayer: heard. The verse displays the blessing: temporal. The verse displays the princes: twelve. The verse displays the nation: great. The blessing displays Ishmael blessed for Abraham's sake. The verse displays the Ishmael: blessed. The verse displays the princes: begotten. The verse displays the nation: great. The blessing of Ishmael in answer to Abraham's prayer displays God's gracious regard: I have heard thee; I have blessed Ishmael, and will multiply him; twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation — Ishmael blessed with a numerous posterity and temporal greatness, yet the covenant with Isaac (v.21). The verse displays the blessed Ishmael, but the covenant with Isaac (v.21).
21:
"But my covenant will I establish with Isaac, which Sarah shall bear unto thee at this set time in the next year." The witnesses note the covenant established with Isaac, born at the set time. On "my covenant will I establish with Isaac," the grammatical voices note the covenant-line. Gill and JFB note that "my covenant will I establish with Isaac" — God fixed the covenant (and the line of promise) with Isaac, not Ishmael; the "but" marks the distinction — Ishmael blessed (v.20), but the covenant with Isaac; the covenant-line, through which the promised seed and the Messiah would come, established in Isaac (Romans 9:7-9; Galatians 4:28).

On "which Sarah shall bear unto thee," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the mother. Wesley and Gill note that Isaac would be born of Sarah — the son of the wife, the matriarch of the promise; not of Hagar, but of Sarah; the covenant-seed through the appointed mother; the distinction between the son of the bondwoman (Ishmael) and the son of the free (Isaac, Galatians 4:22-31).

On "at this set time in the next year," the grammatical-historical note the time: "at this set time in the next year" — God fixed the very time of Isaac's birth — at the set time, the following year (fulfilled, 21:2); the precise promise; the son of promise to be born at God's appointed time; the certainty and the timing of the fulfillment. The verse displays the covenant established with Isaac.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the covenant-line — "my covenant will I establish with Isaac," the covenant with Isaac, not Ishmael (Romans 9:7-9, Gill, JFB); "which Sarah shall bear unto thee," Isaac the son of Sarah, the free (Galatians 4:22-31, Wesley, Gill); "at this set time in the next year," the appointed time of birth (21:2, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant with Isaac. The verse marks the covenant: with Isaac. The verse displays the Sarah: shall bear. The verse displays the set time: next year. The verse displays the line: of promise. The covenant displays Isaac the appointed covenant-heir. The verse displays the Isaac: the heir. The verse displays the Sarah: the mother. The verse displays the time: set. The covenant established with Isaac displays the fixing of the line and the time: my covenant will I establish with Isaac, whom Sarah shall bear at this set time next year — the covenant-line through Isaac, the son of Sarah (the free, Galatians 4), to be born at God's appointed time (21:2). The verse displays the covenant with Isaac, as God goes up (v.22).
22:
"And he left off talking with him, and God went up from Abraham." The very brief verse notes the close of God's communion with Abraham. The grammatical voices note the departure. Gill and Calvin note that "he left off talking with him, and God went up from Abraham" — God ended his communication and went up (ascended, withdrew the visible manifestation) from Abraham; the close of the theophany and the covenant-revelation (vv.1-21); "went up" marking the withdrawal of the divine appearance; the conclusion of God's gracious communing with the patriarch.

On "he left off talking with him," the witnesses note the close. Gill notes that God "left off talking" — the communication ended; the covenant fully declared (the names changed, the sign appointed, Isaac promised); the close of the revelation, leaving Abraham to obey (v.23).

On "God went up from Abraham," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ascension: "God went up from Abraham" — the visible appearance ascended and withdrew (as in other theophanies, 35:13); the manifestation ended; God, having revealed the covenant, departed; the patriarch left to carry out the command (the circumcision of his house, v.23); the close of the divine appearing. The verse displays the close of God's communion with Abraham.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the close — "he left off talking with him, and God went up from Abraham," the end of the communication and the withdrawal of the appearance (Gill, Calvin); "he left off talking with him," the covenant fully declared (Gill); "God went up from Abraham," the ascending of the visible manifestation (35:13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the departed God. The verse marks the talking: ended. The verse displays the God: went up. The verse displays the revelation: closed. The verse displays the Abraham: to obey. The close displays God withdrawing after the covenant-revelation. The verse displays the communion: ended. The verse displays the appearance: withdrawn. The verse displays the obedience: to follow. The close of God's communion with Abraham displays the end of the revelation: God left off talking and went up from Abraham — the covenant fully declared, the visible manifestation withdrawn, the patriarch left to carry out the command. The verse displays the departed God, obeyed by Abraham (v.23).
23:
"And Abraham took Ishmael his son, and all that were born in his house, and all that were bought with his money, every male among the men of Abraham's house; and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him." The witnesses note Abraham's prompt obedience in circumcising his house. On "Abraham took Ishmael his son, and all that were born in his house, and all that were bought with his money... and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin," the grammatical voices note the obedience. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abraham took Ishmael his son... every male among the men of Abraham's house; and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin" — Abraham circumcised all the males of his household (Ishmael, the home-born, the bought servants) as God commanded (vv.10-13); the patriarch's full, exact obedience to the covenant-command.

On "in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the promptness. Henry and Gill note that Abraham did it "in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him" — without delay, the very day God commanded; the prompt, immediate obedience of faith; the patriarch not deferring the hard command (the circumcision of himself, aged 99, and his whole house) but obeying at once; the readiness of Abraham's faith.

On the obedience, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the faith: Abraham's immediate, complete obedience — circumcising himself and his whole household the same day — was the obedience of faith; he received the sign of the covenant promptly and fully; the pattern of ready obedience to God's command; the faith that obeys at once and entirely. The verse displays Abraham's prompt obedience in circumcising his house.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Abraham took Ishmael his son... and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin," Abraham circumcising all the males of his house (vv.10-13, Keil, Gill); "in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him," the prompt, same-day obedience (Henry, Gill); the immediate, complete obedience of faith (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the obedient Abraham. The verse marks the circumcision: of all the males. The verse displays the day: the selfsame. The verse displays the obedience: prompt. The verse displays the faith: ready. The obedience displays Abraham circumcising his house at once. The verse displays the Abraham: obeying. The verse displays the house: circumcised. The verse displays the day: same. Abraham's prompt obedience in circumcising his house displays the obedience of faith: Abraham circumcised Ishmael and all the males of his house in the selfsame day, as God had said — the patriarch's immediate, complete obedience to the covenant-command, the readiness of faith. The verse displays the obedient Abraham, circumcised at 99 (vv.24-25).
24:
"And Abraham was ninety years old and nine, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin." The very brief verse notes Abraham's age at his circumcision. The grammatical voices note the age. Gill notes that "Abraham was ninety years old and nine (ninety-nine), when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin" — Abraham received the covenant-sign at the age of ninety-nine; the patriarch, advanced in years, obeyed the command and was circumcised; the dating of his circumcision, the same day God commanded (v.23).

On "ninety years old and nine," the witnesses note the chronology. Gill notes that Abraham was ninety-nine at his circumcision (the same year as the covenant, v.1); the late reception of the sign (long after his justification by faith, 15:6, twenty-four years before; so that, as Paul argues, Romans 4:10-11, Abraham was justified by faith while uncircumcised, and circumcision was the seal of a righteousness he already had).

On the age, the reformed and grammatical-historical note Paul's argument: Abraham's circumcision at ninety-nine, long after his justification (15:6), is the ground of Paul's argument (Romans 4:10-12) that righteousness is by faith, not by circumcision — Abraham being justified while yet uncircumcised, the father of believing Gentiles as well as Jews; the chronology serving the doctrine of justification by faith. The verse displays Abraham's age at his circumcision.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the age — "Abraham was ninety years old and nine, when he was circumcised," Abraham circumcised at ninety-nine (Gill); the late reception of the sign, long after his justification (15:6, Gill); Paul's argument that Abraham was justified while uncircumcised (Romans 4:10-12, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the circumcised Abraham. The verse marks the age: 99. The verse displays the circumcision: received. The verse displays the justification: prior (15:6). The verse displays the faith: before the sign. The age displays Abraham circumcised long after his justification. The verse displays the Abraham: 99. The verse displays the sign: received. The verse displays the faith: first. Abraham's age at his circumcision displays the chronology that grounds justification by faith: Abraham was ninety-nine when he was circumcised — receiving the sign long after his justification by faith (15:6), so that (Romans 4:10-12) circumcision was the seal of a righteousness he already had by faith. The verse displays the circumcised Abraham, with Ishmael at thirteen (v.25).
25:
"And Ishmael his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin." The very brief verse notes Ishmael's age at his circumcision. The grammatical voices note the age. Gill and Ellicott note that "Ishmael his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin" — Ishmael was thirteen when he was circumcised with the household (v.23); the son born thirteen years before (16:16) now received the sign; the dating of Ishmael's circumcision.

On "thirteen years old," the witnesses note the age. Gill notes that Ishmael was thirteen; (the Arabs, his descendants, are said to circumcise at thirteen, after the example of Ishmael); the son circumcised at thirteen, with Abraham (99) and the household; the age noted.

On Ishmael's circumcision, the grammatical-historical note the household: Ishmael, with Abraham and all the males of the house (vv.23, 27), received the covenant-sign; the whole household circumcised together; Ishmael, though not the covenant-heir (v.21), yet circumcised as a member of Abraham's house; the sign borne by all the males. The verse displays Ishmael's age at his circumcision.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the age — "Ishmael his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised," Ishmael circumcised at thirteen (16:16; v.23, Gill, Ellicott); thirteen, the age of the Arabs' circumcision after Ishmael (Gill); Ishmael circumcised with the household, though not the covenant-heir (v.21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the circumcised Ishmael. The verse marks the age: 13. The verse displays the Ishmael: circumcised. The verse displays the household: together. The verse displays the sign: borne. The age displays Ishmael circumcised at thirteen with the house. The verse displays the Ishmael: 13. The verse displays the sign: received. The verse displays the house: circumcised. Ishmael's age at his circumcision displays the household sign: Ishmael was thirteen when he was circumcised — the son, born thirteen years before (16:16), receiving the covenant-sign with Abraham and the household, though not the covenant-heir (v.21). The verse displays the circumcised Ishmael, with all the house (vv.26-27).
26:
"In the selfsame day was Abraham circumcised, and Ishmael his son." The very brief verse notes Abraham and Ishmael circumcised the same day. The grammatical voices note the obedience. Gill and Ellicott note that "in the selfsame day was Abraham circumcised, and Ishmael his son" — Abraham and Ishmael (and the whole household, v.27) were circumcised on the very day God commanded (v.23); the prompt, same-day obedience; the patriarch and his son receiving the sign together without delay; the immediate fulfillment of the command.

On "in the selfsame day," the witnesses note the promptness. Gill notes that it was done "in the selfsame day" — the very day of the command (v.23); the readiness of Abraham's obedience; no delay in the hard and humbling command; the faith that obeys at once.

On Abraham and Ishmael, the grammatical-historical note the together: "Abraham... and Ishmael his son" — the father and son circumcised together; the household head and his firstborn receiving the sign on the same day; the covenant-sign borne by Abraham (99) and Ishmael (13) alike; the patriarch leading his house in obedience. The verse displays Abraham and Ishmael circumcised the same day.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "in the selfsame day was Abraham circumcised, and Ishmael his son," father and son circumcised the same day (v.23, Gill, Ellicott); "in the selfsame day," the prompt obedience (Gill); Abraham and Ishmael circumcised together (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the circumcised pair. The verse marks the day: the selfsame. The verse displays the Abraham: circumcised. The verse displays the Ishmael: with him. The verse displays the obedience: prompt. The obedience displays father and son circumcised together at once. The verse displays the Abraham: obeying. The verse displays the Ishmael: circumcised. The verse displays the day: same. Abraham and Ishmael circumcised the same day display the prompt obedience: in the selfsame day were Abraham and Ishmael circumcised — the patriarch and his son receiving the covenant-sign together, the very day God commanded, the readiness of faith. The verse displays the circumcised pair, with all the household (v.27).
27:
"And all the men of his house, born in the house, and bought with money of the stranger, were circumcised with him." The very brief verse notes the whole household circumcised with Abraham. The grammatical voices note the completeness. Gill notes that "all the men of his house, born in the house, and bought with money of the stranger, were circumcised with him" — all the males of Abraham's household (the home-born servants and the bought slaves) were circumcised with Abraham; the complete obedience to the command (vv.12-13); the whole household brought into the covenant by the sign; not a male left uncircumcised.

On "born in the house, and bought with money of the stranger," the witnesses note the household. Gill notes that both the home-born and the purchased servants (even those not of Abraham's blood) were circumcised; the whole household of faith included; the sign embracing all the males of the house, natural seed and servants alike.

On "were circumcised with him," the grammatical-historical note the unity: "were circumcised with him" — the whole household circumcised together with Abraham; the patriarch and all his males receiving the sign; the complete, exact fulfillment of God's command (vv.10-13); the covenant-people, in Abraham's house, marked with the sign; the obedience of faith carried out fully. The verse displays the whole household circumcised with Abraham.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the completeness — "all the men of his house, born in the house, and bought with money of the stranger, were circumcised with him," the whole household circumcised (vv.12-13, Gill); both home-born and bought servants included (Gill); "were circumcised with him," the complete obedience, all the males marked (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the circumcised household. The verse marks the household: all the males. The verse displays the home-born: and bought. The verse displays the circumcision: with Abraham. The verse displays the obedience: complete. The completeness displays the whole house marked with the covenant-sign. The verse displays the household: circumcised. The verse displays the sign: borne by all. The verse displays the obedience: full. The whole household circumcised with Abraham displays the complete obedience: all the men of his house, home-born and bought, were circumcised with him — the whole household brought into the covenant by the sign, the patriarch's full, exact obedience to God's command. The verse closes the covenant chapter, before the appearing at Mamre (ch.18).
